6	//! Asynchronous copy intrinsics (`cp.async`).
7	//!
8	//! These intrinsics perform asynchronous copies from global memory to shared
9	//! memory using the `.ca` (cache-all-levels) cache policy.
10	//!
11	//! # Variants
12	//!
13	//! | Function                   | Bytes | Zero-fill | PTX                                                    |
14	//! |----------------------------|-------|-----------|--------------------------------------------------------|
15	//! | [`cp_async_ca_4`]          | 4     | No        | `cp.async.ca.shared.global [smem], [gmem], 4;`         |
16	//! | [`cp_async_ca_8`]          | 8     | No        | `cp.async.ca.shared.global [smem], [gmem], 8;`         |
17	//! | [`cp_async_ca_zfill_4`]    | 4     | Yes       | `cp.async.ca.shared.global [smem], [gmem], 4, src_sz;` |
18	//! | [`cp_async_ca_zfill_8`]    | 8     | Yes       | `cp.async.ca.shared.global [smem], [gmem], 8, src_sz;` |
19	//! | [`cp_async_ca_zfill_16`]   | 16    | Yes       | `cp.async.ca.shared.global [smem], [gmem],16, src_sz;` |
20	//!
21	//! # Notes
22	//!
23	//! The `.cg` (cache-global) cache policy is only supported for 16-byte copies,
24	//! so only `.ca` variants are provided here.
25	//!
26	//! The zero-fill variants copy `src_size` bytes from global memory and
27	//! zero-fill the remaining `cp_size - src_size` bytes in shared memory.
28	//! This is useful for boundary tiles in tiled algorithms where the last
29	//! tile may be smaller than the full tile size.
30	//! CUDA's shipped pipeline and CCCL helpers use `src_size == cp_size` for the
31	//! full-copy case, so the accepted range is `0..=cp_size`; larger values are
32	//! invalid.
33	//!
34	//! The functions are compiler-recognized stubs. Their bodies never execute; the
35	//! cuda-oxide compiler replaces each call with the corresponding PTX instruction.
36	
37	/// Asynchronous 4-byte copy from global to shared memory with `.ca` cache policy.
38	///
39	/// Initiates an asynchronous copy of 4 bytes from global memory (`global_src`)
40	/// to shared memory (`shared_dst`) using the cache-all-levels (`.ca`) policy.
41	///
42	/// # PTX Instruction
43	///
44	/// `cp.async.ca.shared.global [shared_dst], [global_src], 4;`
45	///
46	/// # Safety
47	///
48	/// - `shared_dst` must point to 4 writable bytes in shared memory.
49	/// - `global_src` must point to 4 readable bytes in global memory.
50	/// - Both pointers must be aligned to 4 bytes.
51	/// - Both memory ranges must remain valid, and `global_src` must not be
52	///   modified, until the copy completes.
53	/// - `shared_dst` must not be read or written, including by an overlapping
54	///   asynchronous copy in the same group, until the copy completes.
55	/// - Before accessing the destination, the caller must complete this copy with
56	///   `cp.async.wait_all`, `cp.async.commit_group` followed by a matching
57	///   `cp.async.wait_group`, or an mbarrier that tracks this operation.
58	/// - Group waits cover copies issued by the executing thread. If another thread
59	///   will access the destination, synchronize the threads after completion.
60	/// - Completion instructions emitted with [`ptx_asm!`](crate::ptx_asm) must use
61	///   `clobber("memory")` so the compiler cannot move memory accesses across the
62	///   wait.
63	///
64	/// # See also
65	///
66	/// - [`cp_async_ca_8`]: 8-byte variant.
67	/// - [`cp_async_ca_zfill_4`]: 4-byte variant with zero-fill.
68	#[inline(never)]
69	pub unsafe fn cp_async_ca_4(_shared_dst: *mut u32, _global_src: *const u32) {
70	    // Lowered to inline PTX: cp.async.ca.shared.global [shared_dst], [global_src], 4;
71	    unreachable!("cp_async_ca_4 called outside CUDA kernel context")
72	}
73

75	///
76	/// Initiates an asynchronous copy of 8 bytes from global memory (`global_src`)

78	///

80	///
81	/// `cp.async.ca.shared.global [shared_dst], [global_src], 8;`

83	/// # Safety

86	/// - `global_src` must point to 8 readable bytes in global memory.
87	/// - Both pointers must be aligned to 8 bytes.

89	///   modified, until the copy completes.
90	/// - `shared_dst` must not be read or written, including by an overlapping

92	/// - Before accessing the destination, the caller must complete this copy with

94	///   `cp.async.wait_group`, or an mbarrier that tracks this operation.

98	///   `clobber("memory")` so the compiler cannot move memory accesses across the
99	///   wait.
100	///
101	/// # See also
102	///
103	/// - [`cp_async_ca_4`]: 4-byte variant.
104	/// - [`cp_async_ca_zfill_8`]: 8-byte variant with zero-fill.
105	#[inline(never)]
106	pub unsafe fn cp_async_ca_8(_shared_dst: *mut u32, _global_src: *const u32) {
107	    // Lowered to inline PTX: cp.async.ca.shared.global [shared_dst], [global_src], 8;
108	    unreachable!("cp_async_ca_8 called outside CUDA kernel context")
109	}

111	// =============================================================================
112	// cp.async with zero-fill (src_size parameter)
113	// =============================================================================
114	
115	/// Asynchronous 4-byte copy from global to shared memory with zero-fill.
116	///
117	/// Copies `src_size` bytes from global memory to shared memory and
118	/// zero-fills the remaining `4 - src_size` bytes. When `src_size == 4`,
119	/// this behaves identically to [`cp_async_ca_4`].
120	///
121	/// # PTX Instruction
122	///
123	/// `cp.async.ca.shared.global [shared_dst], [global_src], 4, src_size;`
124	///
125	/// # Safety
126	///
127	/// - `src_size` must be at most 4.
128	/// - `shared_dst` must point to 4 writable bytes in shared memory.
129	/// - `global_src` must be a global-memory pointer whose first `src_size` bytes
130	///   are readable.
131	/// - Both pointers must be aligned to 4 bytes.
132	/// - Both memory ranges must remain valid, and the readable prefix at
133	///   `global_src` must not be modified, until the copy completes.
134	/// - No operation may read or write any byte in the destination range,
135	///   including through an overlapping asynchronous copy, until this copy
136	///   completes.
137	/// - The thread issuing the copy must complete it with `cp.async.wait_all`,
138	///   `cp.async.commit_group` followed by a matching `cp.async.wait_group`, or an
139	///   mbarrier that tracks this operation. These waits cover only copies issued
140	///   by that thread.
141	/// - User-authored completion assembly must include a compiler memory clobber
142	///   (for example, `ptx_asm!(..., clobber("memory"))`) so memory accesses cannot
143	///   move across the completion wait.
144	/// - If another thread will access the destination, synchronize the threads
145	///   after the issuing thread has completed the copy.
146	///
147	/// # See also
148	///
149	/// - [`cp_async_ca_4`]: 4-byte copy without zero-fill.
150	/// - [`cp_async_ca_zfill_8`]: 8-byte variant.
151	/// - [`cp_async_ca_zfill_16`]: 16-byte variant.
152	#[inline(never)]
153	pub unsafe fn cp_async_ca_zfill_4(_shared_dst: *mut u32, _global_src: *const u8, _src_size: u32) {
154	    unreachable!("cp_async_ca_zfill_4 called outside CUDA kernel context")
155	}
156	
157	/// Asynchronous 8-byte copy from global to shared memory with zero-fill.
158	///
159	/// Copies `src_size` bytes from global memory to shared memory and
160	/// zero-fills the remaining `8 - src_size` bytes. When `src_size == 8`,
161	/// this behaves identically to [`cp_async_ca_8`].
162	///
163	/// # PTX Instruction
164	///
165	/// `cp.async.ca.shared.global [shared_dst], [global_src], 8, src_size;`
166	///
167	/// # Safety
168	///
169	/// - `src_size` must be at most 8.
170	/// - `shared_dst` must point to 8 writable bytes in shared memory.
171	/// - `global_src` must be a global-memory pointer whose first `src_size` bytes
172	///   are readable.
173	/// - Both pointers must be aligned to 8 bytes.
174	/// - Both memory ranges must remain valid, and the readable prefix at

179	/// - The thread issuing the copy must complete it with `cp.async.wait_all`,
180	///   `cp.async.commit_group` followed by a matching `cp.async.wait_group`, or an

182	///   by that thread.
183	/// - User-authored completion assembly must include a compiler memory clobber

185	///   move across the completion wait.
186	/// - If another thread will access the destination, synchronize the threads

188	///
189	/// # See also
190	///
191	/// - [`cp_async_ca_8`]: 8-byte copy without zero-fill.
192	/// - [`cp_async_ca_zfill_4`]: 4-byte variant.
193	/// - [`cp_async_ca_zfill_16`]: 16-byte variant.
194	#[inline(never)]
195	pub unsafe fn cp_async_ca_zfill_8(_shared_dst: *mut u32, _global_src: *const u8, _src_size: u32) {
196	    unreachable!("cp_async_ca_zfill_8 called outside CUDA kernel context")

1	/*
2	 * SPDX-FileCopyrightText: Copyright (c) 2026 NVIDIA CORPORATION & AFFILIATES. All rights reserved.

6	//! GPU atomic operations with explicit scope and memory ordering.
7	//!
8	//! This module provides atomic types for CUDA device code. Unlike
9	//! `core::sync::atomic`, these types have explicit **scope** (which threads
10	//! see the atomic) baked into the type, and all memory orderings map directly
11	//! to PTX ordering qualifiers.
12	//!
13	//! # Scope
14	//!
15	//! | Prefix           | PTX scope | Which threads observe it            |
16	//! |------------------|-----------|-------------------------------------|
17	//! | `DeviceAtomic*`  | `.gpu`    | All threads on the GPU (device)     |
18	//! | `BlockAtomic*`   | `.cta`    | Same thread block only              |
19	//! | `SystemAtomic*`  | `.sys`    | GPU **and** CPU (unified memory)    |
20	//!
21	//! # Ordering
22	//!
23	//! All five standard orderings are supported:
24	//!
25	//! | Variant   | PTX qualifier | Cost     |
26	//! |-----------|---------------|----------|
27	//! | `Relaxed` | `.relaxed`    | Cheapest |
28	//! | `Acquire` | `.acquire`    | Low      |
29	//! | `Release` | `.release`    | Low      |
30	//! | `AcqRel`  | `.acq_rel`    | Medium   |
31	//! | `SeqCst`  | `fence.sc` + op | Highest  |
32	//!
33	//! # Types
34	//!
35	//! | Type                         | Operations                                 |
36	//! |------------------------------|--------------------------------------------|
37	//! | Integer (U32, I32, U64, I64) | load, store, all RMW ops, compare_exchange |
38	//! | Float (F16, F32, F64)        | load, store, fetch_add, fetch_sub, swap    |
39	//!
40	//! Float atomics do **not** support compare_exchange (PTX has no `atom.cas`
41	//! for float types) or bitwise operations.
42	//!
43	//! # Example
44	//!
45	//! ```rust,ignore
46	//! use cuda_device::atomic::{DeviceAtomicU32, AtomicOrdering};
47	//!
48	//! #[kernel]
49	//! fn histogram(data: &[u32], bins: &mut [DeviceAtomicU32]) {
50	//!     let idx = cuda_device::thread::index_1d();
51	//!     let val = data[idx];
52	//!     bins[val as usize].fetch_add(1, AtomicOrdering::Relaxed);
53	//! }
54	//! ```
55	//!
56	//! # Naming and overlap with `core::sync::atomic`
57	//!
58	//! Device-scope types are named **`DeviceAtomic*`** (e.g. `DeviceAtomicU32`) so they
59	//! do not collide with `core::sync::atomic::AtomicU32`. Block and system scopes
60	//! keep the `BlockAtomic*` and `SystemAtomic*` prefixes.
61	//!
62	//! The ordering enum is **`AtomicOrdering`** (not `Ordering`) so you can use
63	//! `Ordering` from the standard library without a name clash. For example:
64	//! `use core::sync::atomic::{AtomicU32, Ordering};` for host/cpu code, and
65	//! `use cuda_device::atomic::{DeviceAtomicU32, AtomicOrdering};` for device code.
66	//!
67	//! # Host–device ABI
68	//!
69	//! The ABI is the same for atomics on host and device: `core::sync::atomic::AtomicT`
70	//! and `DeviceAtomicT` / `BlockAtomicT` / `SystemAtomicT` have the same layout
71	//! (a single `T`). The same allocation can be used from host and device in
72	//! unified memory without conversion.
73	//!
74	//! # Implementation
75	//!
76	//! Each method is a stub that the cuda-oxide compiler recognizes by name and
77	//! replaces with the correct LLVM atomic instruction. The method bodies are
78	//! never executed -- they exist only so the type checker and borrow checker
79	//! work normally during development.
80	
81	use core::cell::UnsafeCell;
82	
83	// =============================================================================
84	// Memory Ordering
85	// =============================================================================
86	
87	/// Memory ordering for atomic operations.
88	///
89	/// Mirrors `core::sync::atomic::Ordering` with the same semantics.
90	/// The compiler reads the discriminant value to select the PTX ordering.
91	/// Named `AtomicOrdering` so you can use `core::sync::atomic::Ordering` in
92	/// the same crate without a name clash.
93	///
94	/// Discriminant values (must stay in sync with mir-importer):
95	/// - `Relaxed` = 0
96	/// - `Acquire` = 1
97	/// - `Release` = 2
98	/// - `AcqRel`  = 3
99	/// - `SeqCst`  = 4
100	#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, PartialEq, Eq)]
101	#[repr(u8)]
102	pub enum AtomicOrdering {
103	    /// No ordering guarantees. Cheapest. Use when you only need atomicity
104	    /// (e.g., a counter where you don't care about ordering of other writes).
105	    Relaxed = 0,
106	    /// If I see a released value, I also see everything the releaser wrote
107	    /// before it. Valid for: `load`, `fetch_*`, `compare_exchange`.
108	    Acquire = 1,
109	    /// All my prior writes become visible to whoever acquires this value.
110	    /// Valid for: `store`, `fetch_*`, `compare_exchange`.
111	    Release = 2,
112	    /// Both acquire and release in one operation.
113	    /// Valid for: `fetch_*`, `compare_exchange`.
114	    AcqRel = 3,
115	    /// All the above, plus a single global total order. Most expensive.
116	    /// Valid for: all operations.
117	    SeqCst = 4,
118	}
119	
120	// =============================================================================
121	// Macros for generating atomic types
122	// =============================================================================
123	
124	/// Defines an integer atomic type with all supported operations.
125	///
126	/// Generated methods:
127	/// - `new(val)` — constructor
128	/// - `from_ptr(ptr)` — non-owning view over existing `*mut T` memory
129	/// - `load`, `store` — atomic load/store
130	/// - `fetch_add`, `fetch_sub` — arithmetic RMW
131	/// - `fetch_and`, `fetch_or`, `fetch_xor` — bitwise RMW
132	/// - `fetch_min`, `fetch_max` — comparison RMW
133	/// - `swap` — atomic exchange
134	/// - `compare_exchange_raw` (private) — raw CAS intrinsic returning T
135	/// - `compare_exchange` — CAS returning `Result<T, T>`
136	///
137	/// All stub methods are `#[inline(never)]` with `unreachable!()` bodies.
138	/// The cuda-oxide compiler intercepts calls by method name and replaces
139	/// them with the appropriate LLVM atomic instructions.
140	macro_rules! define_integer_atomic {
141	    (
142	        $(#[$outer:meta])*
143	        pub struct $Name:ident ($ty:ty);
144	    ) => {
145	        $(#[$outer])*
146	        #[repr(transparent)]
147	        pub struct $Name {
148	            inner: UnsafeCell<$ty>,
149	        }
150	
151	        // SAFETY: Atomic types are specifically designed for shared access.
152	        // All operations go through atomic instructions emitted by the compiler.
153	        unsafe impl Sync for $Name {}
154	
155	        impl $Name {
156	            /// Create a new atomic with the given initial value.
157	            pub const fn new(val: $ty) -> Self {
158	                $Name { inner: UnsafeCell::new(val) }
159	            }
160	
161	            /// Reinterpret a raw pointer as a reference to this atomic.
162	            ///
163	            /// This is the non-owning view pattern: hand it a pointer to an
164	            /// existing plain `T` and use atomic semantics over that location.
165	            /// Mirrors `core::sync::atomic::AtomicU32::from_ptr` in shape and
166	            /// is the closest cuda-oxide equivalent of C++'s
167	            /// `cuda::atomic_ref<T, Scope>` constructor.
168	            ///
169	            /// # Safety
170	            ///
171	            /// * `ptr` must be aligned to `align_of::<Self>()` (which equals
172	            ///   `align_of::<T>()` for this `#[repr(transparent)]` type).
173	            /// * The memory at `ptr` must be valid for reads and writes for
174	            ///   the entire lifetime `'a`.
175	            /// * No non-atomic accesses to `*ptr` may occur during `'a`.
176	            ///   All accesses through the returned reference are atomic; mixing
177	            ///   atomic and non-atomic access to the same location is UB.
178	            /// * For block-scoped types, only threads in the same thread block
179	            ///   may access this memory.
180	            /// * For system-scoped types, the memory must be reachable by both
181	            ///   the GPU and the CPU (unified memory).
182	            #[inline(always)]
183	            pub const unsafe fn from_ptr<'a>(ptr: *mut $ty) -> &'a Self {
184	                // SAFETY: `Self` is `#[repr(transparent)]` over `UnsafeCell<$ty>`,
185	                // which is layout-compatible with `$ty`. The caller upholds all
186	                // alignment, validity, and aliasing invariants.
187	                unsafe { &*(ptr as *const Self) }
188	            }

190	            // ── Load / Store ───────────────────────────────────────────
191	
192	            /// Atomically load the value.
193	            ///
194	            /// `order` must be `Relaxed`, `Acquire`, or `SeqCst`.
195	            #[inline(never)]
196	            pub fn load(&self, order: AtomicOrdering) -> $ty {
197	                let _ = order;
198	                unreachable!(concat!(stringify!($Name), "::load called outside CUDA kernel context"))
199	            }

201	            /// Atomically store a value.
202	            ///
203	            /// `order` must be `Relaxed`, `Release`, or `SeqCst`.
204	            #[inline(never)]
205	            pub fn store(&self, val: $ty, order: AtomicOrdering) {
206	                let _ = (val, order);
207	                unreachable!(concat!(stringify!($Name), "::store called outside CUDA kernel context"))
208	            }
209	
210	            // ── Arithmetic RMW ─────────────────────────────────────────
211	
212	            /// Atomically add `val` and return the **previous** value.
213	            #[inline(never)]
214	            pub fn fetch_add(&self, val: $ty, order: AtomicOrdering) -> $ty {
215	                let _ = (val, order);
216	                unreachable!(concat!(stringify!($Name), "::fetch_add called outside CUDA kernel context"))
217	            }
218	
219	            /// Atomically subtract `val` and return the **previous** value.
220	            #[inline(never)]
221	            pub fn fetch_sub(&self, val: $ty, order: AtomicOrdering) -> $ty {
222	                let _ = (val, order);
223	                unreachable!(concat!(stringify!($Name), "::fetch_sub called outside CUDA kernel context"))
224	            }
225	
226	            // ── Bitwise RMW ────────────────────────────────────────────
227	
228	            /// Atomically bitwise-AND with `val` and return the **previous** value.
229	            #[inline(never)]
230	            pub fn fetch_and(&self, val: $ty, order: AtomicOrdering) -> $ty {
231	                let _ = (val, order);
232	                unreachable!(concat!(stringify!($Name), "::fetch_and called outside CUDA kernel context"))
233	            }

235	            /// Atomically bitwise-OR with `val` and return the **previous** value.
236	            #[inline(never)]
237	            pub fn fetch_or(&self, val: $ty, order: AtomicOrdering) -> $ty {
238	                let _ = (val, order);
239	                unreachable!(concat!(stringify!($Name), "::fetch_or called outside CUDA kernel context"))
240	            }

242	            /// Atomically bitwise-XOR with `val` and return the **previous** value.
243	            #[inline(never)]
244	            pub fn fetch_xor(&self, val: $ty, order: AtomicOrdering) -> $ty {
245	                let _ = (val, order);
246	                unreachable!(concat!(stringify!($Name), "::fetch_xor called outside CUDA kernel context"))
247	            }
248	
249	            // ── Comparison RMW ─────────────────────────────────────────
250	
251	            /// Atomically compute the minimum and return the **previous** value.
252	            #[inline(never)]
253	            pub fn fetch_min(&self, val: $ty, order: AtomicOrdering) -> $ty {
254	                let _ = (val, order);
255	                unreachable!(concat!(stringify!($Name), "::fetch_min called outside CUDA kernel context"))
256	            }
257	
258	            /// Atomically compute the maximum and return the **previous** value.
259	            #[inline(never)]
260	            pub fn fetch_max(&self, val: $ty, order: AtomicOrdering) -> $ty {
261	                let _ = (val, order);
262	                unreachable!(concat!(stringify!($Name), "::fetch_max called outside CUDA kernel context"))
263	            }
264	
265	            // ── Exchange ───────────────────────────────────────────────
266	
267	            /// Atomically swap with `val` and return the **previous** value.
268	            #[inline(never)]
269	            pub fn swap(&self, val: $ty, order: AtomicOrdering) -> $ty {
270	                let _ = (val, order);
271	                unreachable!(concat!(stringify!($Name), "::swap called outside CUDA kernel context"))
272	            }
273	
274	            // ── Compare-and-exchange ───────────────────────────────────
275	
276	            /// Low-level compare-and-swap. Returns the **previous** value.
277	            ///
278	            /// This is the raw intrinsic intercepted by the compiler.
279	            /// Use [`compare_exchange`](Self::compare_exchange) for the
280	            /// `Result`-returning public API.
281	            #[inline(never)]
282	            fn compare_exchange_raw(
283	                &self,
284	                current: $ty,
285	                new: $ty,
286	                success: AtomicOrdering,
287	                failure: AtomicOrdering,
288	            ) -> $ty {
289	                let _ = (current, new, success, failure);
290	                unreachable!(concat!(
291	                    stringify!($Name),
292	                    "::compare_exchange_raw called outside CUDA kernel context"
293	                ))
294	            }
295	
296	            /// Compare-and-swap. If `*self == current`, set `*self = new`
297	            /// and return `Ok(current)`. Otherwise return `Err(actual_value)`.
298	            ///
299	            /// `success` is the ordering for the RMW if the comparison succeeds.
300	            /// `failure` is the ordering for the load if the comparison fails.
301	            #[inline(always)]
302	            pub fn compare_exchange(
303	                &self,
304	                current: $ty,
305	                new: $ty,
306	                success: AtomicOrdering,
307	                failure: AtomicOrdering,
308	            ) -> Result<$ty, $ty> {
309	                let old = self.compare_exchange_raw(current, new, success, failure);
310	                if old == current {
311	                    Ok(old)
312	                } else {
313	                    Err(old)
314	                }
315	            }
316	        }
317	    };
318	}

320	/// Defines a float atomic type with the subset of operations that
321	/// hardware supports for floating-point values.
322	///
323	/// Generated methods:
324	/// - `new(val)` — constructor
325	/// - `from_ptr(ptr)` — non-owning view over existing `*mut T` memory
326	/// - `load`, `store` — atomic load/store
327	/// - `fetch_add`, `fetch_sub` — arithmetic RMW
328	/// - `swap` — atomic exchange (`atomicrmw xchg`)
329	///
330	/// **Not supported** (PTX hardware limitation):
331	/// - `compare_exchange` — no `atom.cas` for float types
332	/// - `fetch_and/or/xor` — bitwise ops meaningless on floats
333	/// - `fetch_min/max` — not in initial implementation
334	macro_rules! define_float_atomic {
335	    (
336	        $(#[$outer:meta])*
337	        pub struct $Name:ident ($ty:ty);
338	    ) => {
339	        $(#[$outer])*
340	        #[repr(transparent)]
341	        pub struct $Name {
342	            inner: UnsafeCell<$ty>,
343	        }
344	
345	        // SAFETY: Atomic types are specifically designed for shared access.
346	        // All operations go through atomic instructions emitted by the compiler.
347	        unsafe impl Sync for $Name {}
348	
349	        impl $Name {
350	            /// Create a new atomic with the given initial value.
351	            pub const fn new(val: $ty) -> Self {
352	                $Name { inner: UnsafeCell::new(val) }
353	            }
354	
355	            /// Reinterpret a raw pointer as a reference to this atomic.
356	            ///
357	            /// See the matching method on the integer atomic types for the
358	            /// full safety contract; the rules are identical for floats.
359	            ///
360	            /// # Safety
361	            ///
362	            /// * `ptr` must be aligned to `align_of::<Self>()`.
363	            /// * The memory at `ptr` must be valid for reads and writes for
364	            ///   the entire lifetime `'a`.
365	            /// * No non-atomic accesses to `*ptr` may occur during `'a`.
366	            /// * Block-scoped: only same-block threads may access.
367	            ///   System-scoped: memory must be reachable by GPU and CPU.
368	            #[inline(always)]
369	            pub const unsafe fn from_ptr<'a>(ptr: *mut $ty) -> &'a Self {
370	                // SAFETY: `Self` is `#[repr(transparent)]` over `UnsafeCell<$ty>`,
371	                // which is layout-compatible with `$ty`. The caller upholds all
372	                // alignment, validity, and aliasing invariants.
373	                unsafe { &*(ptr as *const Self) }
374	            }
375	
376	            // ── Load / Store ───────────────────────────────────────────
377	
378	            /// Atomically load the value.
379	            ///
380	            /// `order` must be `Relaxed`, `Acquire`, or `SeqCst`.
381	            #[inline(never)]
382	            pub fn load(&self, order: AtomicOrdering) -> $ty {
383	                let _ = order;
384	                unreachable!(concat!(stringify!($Name), "::load called outside CUDA kernel context"))
385	            }
386	
387	            /// Atomically store a value.
388	            ///
389	            /// `order` must be `Relaxed`, `Release`, or `SeqCst`.
390	            #[inline(never)]
391	            pub fn store(&self, val: $ty, order: AtomicOrdering) {
392	                let _ = (val, order);
393	                unreachable!(concat!(stringify!($Name), "::store called outside CUDA kernel context"))
394	            }
395	
396	            // ── Arithmetic RMW ─────────────────────────────────────────
397	
398	            /// Atomically add `val` and return the **previous** value.
399	            ///
400	            /// Uses LLVM `atomicrmw fadd`.
401	            #[inline(never)]
402	            pub fn fetch_add(&self, val: $ty, order: AtomicOrdering) -> $ty {
403	                let _ = (val, order);
404	                unreachable!(concat!(stringify!($Name), "::fetch_add called outside CUDA kernel context"))
405	            }
406	
407	            /// Atomically subtract `val` and return the **previous** value.
408	            ///
409	            /// Lowered as `atomicrmw fadd` of the negated value, so the
410	            /// backend can keep using native PTX add atomics.
411	            #[inline(never)]
412	            pub fn fetch_sub(&self, val: $ty, order: AtomicOrdering) -> $ty {
413	                let _ = (val, order);
414	                unreachable!(concat!(stringify!($Name), "::fetch_sub called outside CUDA kernel context"))
415	            }
416	
417	            // ── Exchange ───────────────────────────────────────────────
418	
419	            /// Atomically swap with `val` and return the **previous** value.
420	            #[inline(never)]
421	            pub fn swap(&self, val: $ty, order: AtomicOrdering) -> $ty {
422	                let _ = (val, order);
423	                unreachable!(concat!(stringify!($Name), "::swap called outside CUDA kernel context"))
424	            }
425	        }
426	    };
427	}

4	//! Packed `bf16x2` arithmetic intrinsics.
5	//!
6	//! Ampere (`sm_80+`) supports packed FMA, min, max, negation, and absolute
7	//! value. Packed add, subtract, and multiply were added for Hopper (`sm_90+`).
8	//! On Ampere, callers can still express add and multiply through FMA with a
9	//! packed `1.0` or `0.0` operand.
10	//!
11	//! Each `u32` carries two bf16 values: low 16 bits = first lane, high 16 bits
12	//! = second lane. This matches the layout produced by
13	//! [`crate::tcgen05::cvt_f32x2_bf16x2`].

16	///
17	/// All three operands and the result are packed `bf16x2` carried as `u32`,
18	/// matching `cvt.rn.bf16x2.f32`'s output layout (low 16 = first lane, high 16
19	/// = second lane).
20	///
21	/// # PTX
22	///
23	/// ```ptx

6	//! Cluster Launch Control (CLC) intrinsics for Blackwell+ (SM 100+).
7	//!
8	//! CLC replaces software atomic counters for persistent kernel tile scheduling.
9	//! Instead of a fixed number of persistent CTAs competing on a global counter,
10	//! CLC uses hardware-managed work-stealing: launch a full grid (one CTA per tile),
11	//! and running CTAs call `try_cancel` to steal not-yet-launched tiles.
12	//!
13	//! # How It Works
14	//!
15	//! ```text
16	//! ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
17	//! │  Hardware Scheduler                                     │
18	//! │       │                                                 │
19	//! │       │  Launches CTAs as SMs free up                   │
20	//! │       ▼                                                 │
21	//! │  ┌─────────────┐   try_cancel    ┌───────────────────┐  │
22	//! │  │  Running CTA│───────────────► │ Pending CTA queue │  │
23	//! │  │  (wants more│◄─────────────── │ (not yet launched)│  │
24	//! │  │   work)     │  16B response   │                   │  │
25	//! │  └─────────────┘                 └───────────────────┘  │
26	//! │       │                                                 │
27	//! │       │ query_cancel                                    │
28	//! │       ▼                                                 │
29	//! │  is_canceled? → if yes: use ctaid as next tile          │
30	//! │               → if no: exit (no more work)              │
31	//! └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
32	//! ```
33	//!
34	//! # Response Format
35	//!
36	//! `try_cancel` writes a 16-byte (b128) response to shared memory. The response
37	//! is decoded with `query_cancel` variants to extract:
38	//! - Whether a CTA was successfully canceled (i.e., work is available)
39	//! - The grid coordinates (ctaid.x, ctaid.y, ctaid.z) of the canceled CTA
40	//!
41	//! The response is passed as two `u64` values (lo, hi) to the query functions,
42	//! which pack them into a `.b128` PTX register internally.
43	//!
44	//! # Usage Pattern
45	//!
46	//! ```rust,ignore
47	//! use cuda_device::clc::*;
48	//! use cuda_device::barrier::{Barrier, mbarrier_init};
49	//!
50	//! // Shared memory for the 16-byte response and mbarrier
51	//! static mut CLC_RESPONSE: [u64; 2] = [0; 2];
52	//! static mut CLC_BAR: Barrier = Barrier::UNINIT;
53	//!
54	//! // Request next tile
55	//! unsafe {
56	//!     clc_try_cancel(
57	//!         &raw mut CLC_RESPONSE as *mut u8,
58	//!         &raw mut CLC_BAR as *mut u64,
59	//!     );
60	//! }
61	//!
62	//! // Wait for response via mbarrier...
63	//!
64	//! // Decode the response
65	//! let lo = unsafe { CLC_RESPONSE[0] };
66	//! let hi = unsafe { CLC_RESPONSE[1] };
67	//! let canceled = unsafe { clc_query_is_canceled(lo, hi) };
68	//! if canceled != 0 {
69	//!     // Work available — get tile coordinates
70	//!     let tile_x = unsafe { clc_query_get_first_ctaid_x(lo, hi) };
71	//!     let tile_y = unsafe { clc_query_get_first_ctaid_y(lo, hi) };
72	//!     // ... process tile (tile_x, tile_y)
73	//! } else {
74	//!     // No more work — exit
75	//! }
76	//! ```
77	//!
78	//! # Hardware Support
79	//!
80	//! - **PTX ISA 8.6+**
81	//! - **SM 100+ (Blackwell)**
82

6	//! Async barrier primitives for Hopper+ architectures.
7	//!
8	//! Hardware barriers (`mbarrier`) enable efficient synchronization for async
9	//! operations like TMA copies. Unlike `sync_threads()`, barriers can track
10	//! transaction completion asynchronously.
11	//!
12	//! # Architecture
13	//!
14	//! ```text
15	//! ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
16	//! │  TMA (Hardware DMA)                                     │
17	//! │       │                                                 │
18	//! │       │ cp.async.bulk.tensor...                         │
19	//! │       ▼                                                 │
20	//! │  ┌─────────────┐    mbarrier.arrive    ┌─────────────┐  │
21	//! │  │   Shared    │◄─────────────────────►│   Barrier   │  │
22	//! │  │   Memory    │                       │  (64-bit)   │  │
23	//! │  └─────────────┘    mbarrier.wait      └─────────────┘  │
24	//! │       ▲                                      ▲          │
25	//! │       │                                      │          │
26	//! │  Threads read data              Threads check completion│
27	//! └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
28	//! ```
29	//!
30	//! # Usage Pattern
31	//!
32	//! ```rust,ignore
33	//! use cuda_device::{kernel, thread, SharedArray};
34	//! use cuda_device::barrier::{Barrier, mbarrier_init, mbarrier_arrive, mbarrier_wait};
35	//!
36	//! #[kernel]
37	//! pub fn async_copy_kernel(...) {
38	//!     // Barrier in shared memory
39	//!     static mut BAR: Barrier = Barrier::UNINIT;
40	//!
41	//!     // Thread 0 initializes (expected arrivals = block size)
42	//!     if thread::threadIdx_x() == 0 {
43	//!         unsafe { mbarrier_init(&mut BAR, 128); }
44	//!     }
45	//!     thread::sync_threads();
46	//!
47	//!     // ... TMA copy would arrive at barrier when done ...
48	//!
49	//!     // All threads arrive and wait
50	//!     let token = unsafe { mbarrier_arrive(&BAR) };
51	//!     unsafe { mbarrier_wait(&BAR, token); }
52	//!
53	//!     // Barrier phase complete - safe to read data
54	//! }
55	//! ```
56	//!
57	//! # Hardware Support
58	//!
59	//! - **sm_80+ (Ampere)**: Basic mbarrier support
60	//! - **sm_90+ (Hopper)**: Full TMA integration with transaction tracking
61	//! - **sm_120 (Blackwell)**: Enhanced barrier operations
62	
63	// =============================================================================
64	// Barrier Type
65	// =============================================================================
66	
67	/// Hardware barrier for async synchronization.
68	///
69	/// This is a 64-bit value stored in shared memory that tracks:
70	/// - Expected arrival count
71	/// - Current arrival count
72	/// - Phase bit (for reuse across iterations)
73	///
74	/// # Memory Layout (conceptual)
75	///
76	/// ```text
77	/// [63:48] Phase + Hardware State
78	/// [47:32] Expected Arrival Count
79	/// [31:0]  Current Arrival Count
80	/// ```
81	///
82	/// # Safety
83	///
84	/// - Must be declared as `static mut` in shared memory
85	/// - Must be initialized before use with `mbarrier_init`
86	/// - All threads that will arrive must be accounted for in expected count
87	#[repr(C, align(8))]
88	#[derive(Copy, Clone)]
89	pub struct Barrier {
90	    /// Internal 64-bit state managed by hardware
91	    _state: u64,
92	}
93	
94	impl Barrier {
95	    /// Uninitialized barrier constant for `static mut` declarations.
96	    ///
97	    /// # Example
98	    ///
99	    /// ```rust,ignore
100	    /// static mut BAR: Barrier = Barrier::UNINIT;
101	    /// ```
102	    pub const UNINIT: Self = Self { _state: 0 };
103	}
104	
105	// =============================================================================
106	// Barrier Initialization
107	// =============================================================================
108	
109	/// Initialize a barrier with the expected number of arrivals.
110	///
111	/// Must be called by exactly ONE thread before any other barrier operations.
112	/// Typically thread 0 initializes, then `sync_threads()` before use.
113	///
114	/// # Parameters
115	///
116	/// - `bar`: Pointer to barrier in shared memory
117	/// - `expected_count`: Number of threads/transactions that will arrive
118	///
119	/// # Safety
120	///
121	/// - `bar` must point to valid shared memory
122	/// - Must be called exactly once per barrier phase
123	/// - Other threads must not access barrier until init completes
124	///
125	/// # Example
126	///
127	/// ```rust,ignore
128	/// static mut BAR: Barrier = Barrier::UNINIT;
129	///
130	/// if thread::threadIdx_x() == 0 {
131	///     unsafe { mbarrier_init(&mut BAR, 128); }  // 128 threads will arrive
132	/// }
133	/// thread::sync_threads();
134	/// ```
135	///
136	/// # PTX
137	///
138	/// ```ptx
139	/// mbarrier.init.shared.b64 [addr], count;
140	/// ```
141	#[inline(never)]
142	pub unsafe fn mbarrier_init(bar: *mut Barrier, expected_count: u32) {
143	    let _ = (bar, expected_count);
144	    // Lowered to: call void @llvm.nvvm.mbarrier.init.shared(ptr %bar, i32 %count)
145	    unreachable!("mbarrier_init called outside CUDA kernel context")
146	}

148	// =============================================================================
149	// Barrier Arrive Operations
150	// =============================================================================
151	
152	/// Arrive at barrier, signaling this thread's participation.
153	///
154	/// Returns a token (phase) that must be passed to `mbarrier_wait`.
155	/// The barrier completes when all expected arrivals have occurred.
156	///
157	/// # Parameters
158	///
159	/// - `bar`: Pointer to barrier in shared memory
160	///
161	/// # Returns
162	///
163	/// A 64-bit token representing the current barrier phase.
164	///
165	/// # Safety
166	///
167	/// - `bar` must be initialized
168	/// - Must be paired with a `mbarrier_wait` call
169	///
170	/// # Example
171	///
172	/// ```rust,ignore
173	/// let token = unsafe { mbarrier_arrive(&BAR) };
174	/// // ... do independent work ...
175	/// unsafe { mbarrier_wait(&BAR, token); }
176	/// ```
177	///
178	/// # PTX
179	///
180	/// ```ptx
181	/// mbarrier.arrive.shared.b64 token, [addr];
182	/// ```
183	#[inline(never)]
184	pub unsafe fn mbarrier_arrive(bar: *const Barrier) -> u64 {
185	    let _ = bar;
186	    // Lowered to: call i64 @llvm.nvvm.mbarrier.arrive.shared(ptr %bar)
187	    unreachable!("mbarrier_arrive called outside CUDA kernel context")
188	}
189	
190	/// Arrive at barrier without returning a token (fire-and-forget).
191	///
192	/// Use this when the thread will not wait on this barrier.
193	/// For producer threads that signal completion but don't need to wait.
194	///
195	/// # Safety
196	///
197	/// - `bar` must be initialized
198	/// - Only use when this thread will NOT call `mbarrier_wait`
199	///
200	/// # PTX
201	///
202	/// ```ptx
203	/// mbarrier.arrive.noComplete.shared.b64 _, [addr];
204	/// ```
205	#[inline(never)]
206	pub unsafe fn mbarrier_arrive_no_complete(bar: *const Barrier) {
207	    let _ = bar;
208	    // Lowered to: call void @llvm.nvvm.mbarrier.arrive.noComplete.shared(ptr %bar)
209	    unreachable!("mbarrier_arrive_no_complete called outside CUDA kernel context")
210	}

212	// =============================================================================
213	// Barrier Wait Operations
214	// =============================================================================
215	
216	/// Test if barrier phase is complete (non-blocking).
217	///
218	/// Returns `true` if all expected arrivals have occurred for the given phase.
219	/// Use this for polling-style waits or to check completion without blocking.
220	///
221	/// # Parameters
222	///
223	/// - `bar`: Pointer to barrier in shared memory
224	/// - `token`: Phase token from `mbarrier_arrive`
225	///
226	/// # Returns
227	///
228	/// `true` if the barrier phase is complete, `false` otherwise.
229	///
230	/// # Safety
231	///
232	/// - `bar` must be initialized
233	/// - `token` must be from a matching `mbarrier_arrive` call
234	///
235	/// # PTX
236	///
237	/// ```ptx
238	/// mbarrier.test_wait.shared.b64 pred, [addr], token;
239	/// ```
240	#[inline(never)]
241	pub unsafe fn mbarrier_test_wait(bar: *const Barrier, token: u64) -> bool {
242	    let _ = (bar, token);
243	    // Lowered to: call i1 @llvm.nvvm.mbarrier.test_wait.shared(ptr %bar, i64 %token)
244	    unreachable!("mbarrier_test_wait called outside CUDA kernel context")
245	}

247	/// Try to wait for barrier phase to complete (with scheduling hints).
248	///
249	/// Similar to `mbarrier_test_wait` but provides better scheduling hints to the
250	/// hardware. The thread may be suspended briefly while waiting, improving
251	/// efficiency in spin-wait loops.
252	///
253	/// **This is the preferred wait operation for TMA synchronization.** nvcc uses
254	/// this instruction for barrier waits in TMA copy patterns.
255	///
256	/// # Parameters
257	///
258	/// - `bar`: Pointer to barrier in shared memory
259	/// - `token`: Phase token from `mbarrier_arrive`
260	///
261	/// # Returns
262	///
263	/// `true` if the barrier phase is complete, `false` otherwise.
264	///
265	/// # Safety
266	///
267	/// - `bar` must be initialized
268	/// - `token` must be from a matching `mbarrier_arrive` call
269	///
270	/// # Example
271	///
272	/// ```rust,ignore
273	/// let token = unsafe { mbarrier_arrive(&BAR) };
274	/// // Wait with scheduling hints
275	/// while !unsafe { mbarrier_try_wait(&BAR, token) } {
276	///     // Hardware may suspend thread briefly
277	/// }
278	/// ```
279	///
280	/// # PTX
281	///
282	/// ```ptx
283	/// mbarrier.try_wait.shared.b64 pred, [addr], token;
284	/// ```
285	#[inline(never)]
286	pub unsafe fn mbarrier_try_wait(bar: *const Barrier, token: u64) -> bool {
287	    let _ = (bar, token);
288	    // Lowered to inline PTX: mbarrier.try_wait.shared.b64 p, [%bar], %token;
289	    unreachable!("mbarrier_try_wait called outside CUDA kernel context")
290	}

293	///
294	/// This variant is used in patterns where the producer arrives
295	/// via operations that do **not** return a token (e.g. `tcgen05.commit`).
296	///
297	/// # PTX
298	///
299	/// ```ptx
300	/// mbarrier.try_wait.parity.shared::cta.b64 pred, [addr], parity;
301	/// ```
302	///
303	/// # Safety
304	///
305	/// - `bar` must be a valid pointer to a barrier in shared memory
306	/// - Must be called from within a CUDA kernel context
307	#[inline(never)]
308	pub unsafe fn mbarrier_try_wait_parity(bar: *const Barrier, parity: u32) -> bool {
309	    let _ = (bar, parity);
310	    unreachable!("mbarrier_try_wait_parity called outside CUDA kernel context")
311	}

313	/// Try to wait for barrier completion using cluster-scope acquire semantics.
314	///
315	/// This parity-based variant is used by cluster launch-control response reuse,
316	/// where completion must acquire writes made at cluster scope.
317	///
318	/// # PTX
319	///
320	/// ```ptx
321	/// mbarrier.try_wait.parity.acquire.cluster.shared::cta.b64 pred, [addr], parity;
322	/// ```
323	///
324	/// # Safety
325	///
326	/// - `bar` must be a valid pointer to an initialized barrier in CTA shared memory
327	/// - Must be called from within a cluster launch context
328	#[inline(never)]
329	pub unsafe fn mbarrier_try_wait_parity_cluster(bar: *const Barrier, parity: u32) -> bool {
330	    let _ = (bar, parity);
331	    unreachable!("mbarrier_try_wait_parity_cluster called outside CUDA kernel context")
332	}

334	/// Wait for barrier phase to complete (blocking).
335	///
336	/// Blocks until all expected arrivals have occurred for the given phase.
337	/// This is implemented as a loop over `mbarrier_test_wait`.
338	///
339	/// # Parameters
340	///
341	/// - `bar`: Pointer to barrier in shared memory
342	/// - `token`: Phase token from `mbarrier_arrive`
343	///
344	/// # Safety
345	///
346	/// - `bar` must be initialized
347	/// - `token` must be from a matching `mbarrier_arrive` call
348	/// - Calling thread must have arrived at the barrier
349	///
350	/// # Example
351	///
352	/// ```rust,ignore
353	/// let token = unsafe { mbarrier_arrive(&BAR) };
354	/// unsafe { mbarrier_wait(&BAR, token); }
355	/// // Barrier complete - safe to access synchronized data
356	/// ```
357	#[inline(always)]
358	pub unsafe fn mbarrier_wait(bar: *const Barrier, token: u64) {
359	    // Implemented as a spin loop on try_wait.
360	    // try_wait provides scheduling hints to the hardware, making it
361	    // more efficient than test_wait for actual waiting.
362	    while !unsafe { mbarrier_try_wait(bar, token) } {
363	        // spin
364	    }
365	}

367	// =============================================================================
368	// Transaction-Based Barrier Operations (for TMA)
369	// =============================================================================
370	
371	/// Arrive at barrier expecting additional transaction bytes.
372	///
373	/// Used with TMA copies to track async completion. The barrier won't
374	/// complete until BOTH:
375	/// 1. All expected arrivals occur
376	/// 2. All expected transaction bytes are transferred
377	///
378	/// # Parameters
379	///
380	/// - `bar`: Pointer to barrier in shared memory
381	/// - `tx_count`: Number of transactions (typically 1 for single TMA op)
382	/// - `bytes`: Number of bytes expected from async transfers
383	///
384	/// # Returns
385	///
386	/// A 64-bit token representing the current barrier phase.
387	///
388	/// # Safety
389	///
390	/// - `bar` must be initialized with expected arrival count
391	/// - `bytes` must match the actual TMA transfer size
392	///
393	/// # Example (with TMA)
394	///
395	/// ```rust,ignore
396	/// // Thread 0 initiates TMA copy and arrives with transaction
397	/// if thread::threadIdx_x() == 0 {
398	///     tma::copy_async_2d(...);  // TMA will transfer `tile_bytes`
399	///     let token = unsafe { mbarrier_arrive_expect_tx(&BAR, 1, tile_bytes) };
400	/// }
401	///
402	/// // Other threads just arrive
403	/// let token = unsafe { mbarrier_arrive(&BAR) };
404	///
405	/// // All wait for TMA + arrivals
406	/// unsafe { mbarrier_wait(&BAR, token); }
407	/// ```
408	///
409	/// # PTX
410	///
411	/// ```ptx
412	/// mbarrier.arrive.expect_tx.shared.b64 token, [addr], bytes;
413	/// ```
414	#[inline(never)]
415	pub unsafe fn mbarrier_arrive_expect_tx(bar: *const Barrier, _tx_count: u32, bytes: u32) -> u64 {
416	    let _ = (bar, bytes);
417	    // Note: LLVM 20 may not have this intrinsic - may need inline PTX
418	    // Lowered to inline PTX: mbarrier.arrive.expect_tx.shared.b64 %rd, [%bar], %bytes;
419	    unreachable!("mbarrier_arrive_expect_tx called outside CUDA kernel context")
420	}
421	
422	/// Arrive at a CTA-shared barrier with cluster-scope expected transaction bytes.
423	///
424	/// This relaxed arrival is paired with a cluster-scope acquire wait when a
425	/// barrier protects state reused across CTAs in a cluster.
426	///
427	/// # PTX
428	///
429	/// ```ptx
430	/// mbarrier.arrive.expect_tx.relaxed.cluster.shared::cta.b64 token, [addr], bytes;
431	/// ```
432	///
433	/// # Safety
434	///
435	/// - `bar` must point to an initialized barrier in CTA shared memory

441	    _tx_count: u32,
442	    bytes: u32,

448	// =============================================================================
449	// Cluster-Scope Barrier Operations (for TMA Multicast)
450	// =============================================================================
451	
452	/// Arrive at a barrier in another CTA's shared memory within the cluster.
453	///
454	/// This is the key synchronization primitive for TMA multicast. When rank 0
455	/// multicasts B to all CTAs, it must wait for ALL CTAs to consume before
456	/// reusing the buffer. Each CTA's MMA warp calls this to arrive at rank 0's
457	/// consumer barrier (MMA_BAR) via distributed shared memory (DSMEM).
458	///
459	/// Takes a raw `u64` address (from `map_shared_rank` cast to integer) rather
460	/// than a `*const Barrier` to avoid LLVM IR address-space mismatches in loop
461	/// phi nodes. The `mapa` instruction returns a generic-address-space pointer,
462	/// which conflicts with shared-address-space expectations in loop back-edges.
463	/// Using `u64` bypasses the pointer type system entirely.
464	///
465	/// # Usage Pattern (TMA multicast consumer barrier)
466	///
467	/// ```rust,ignore
468	/// // Before the K-loop (once):
469	/// let rank0_mma_bar_addr = unsafe {
470	///     cluster::map_shared_rank(&raw const MMA_BAR0, 0) as u64
471	/// };
472	///
473	/// // Inside the K-loop (after MMA consumes data):
474	/// unsafe { mbarrier_arrive_cluster(rank0_mma_bar_addr); }
475	/// ```
476	///
477	/// # PTX
478	///
479	/// ```ptx
480	/// mbarrier.arrive.release.cluster.shared::cluster.b64 _, [$addr];
481	/// ```
482	///
483	/// # Safety
484	///
485	/// - `remote_bar_addr` must be a valid cluster-scope shared memory address
486	///   obtained from `map_shared_rank` (mapa instruction)
487	/// - The target barrier must be initialized and expecting arrivals
488	/// - Must be called from within a cluster launch context
489	#[inline(never)]
490	pub unsafe fn mbarrier_arrive_cluster(remote_bar_addr: u64) {
491	    let _ = remote_bar_addr;
492	    unreachable!("mbarrier_arrive_cluster called outside CUDA kernel context")
493	}

521	// =============================================================================
522	// Proxy Fence Operations (for TMA synchronization)
523	// =============================================================================
524	
525	/// Fence to synchronize generic proxy with async proxy in shared memory.
526	///
527	/// This fence ensures that memory operations performed through the generic
528	/// proxy (normal thread operations like `mbarrier.init`) are visible to the
529	/// async proxy (hardware async operations like TMA `cp.async.bulk`).
530	///
531	/// **Critical for TMA:** Must be called after `mbarrier_init` and before
532	/// issuing TMA operations. Without this fence, TMA hardware may not see
533	/// the barrier initialization!
534	///
535	/// # Usage Pattern
536	///
537	/// ```rust,ignore
538	/// // Thread 0 initializes barrier
539	/// if thread::threadIdx_x() == 0 {
540	///     unsafe {
541	///         mbarrier_init(&raw mut BAR, count);
542	///         fence_proxy_async_shared_cta();  // <-- REQUIRED for TMA!
543	///     }
544	/// }
545	/// thread::sync_threads();
546	///
547	/// // Now safe to issue TMA operations
548	/// ```
549	///
550	/// # Why This Is Needed
551	///
552	/// NVIDIA GPUs have separate memory "proxies":
553	/// - **Generic Proxy**: Normal thread memory operations
554	/// - **Async Proxy**: Hardware async operations (TMA, cp.async)
555	///
556	/// Without this fence, writes through the generic proxy (like `mbarrier.init`)
557	/// may not be visible to the async proxy when TMA tries to signal the barrier.
558	///
559	/// # Hardware Support
560	///
561	/// - **PTX ISA 8.0+**
562	/// - **sm_90+ (Hopper and newer)**
563	///
564	/// # PTX
565	///
566	/// # Safety
567	///
568	/// Must be called from within a CUDA kernel context.
569	///
570	/// ```ptx
571	/// fence.proxy.async.shared::cta;
572	/// ```
573	#[inline(never)]
574	pub unsafe fn fence_proxy_async_shared_cta() {
575	    // Lowered to inline PTX: fence.proxy.async.shared::cta;
576	    unreachable!("fence_proxy_async_shared_cta called outside CUDA kernel context")
577	}
578

580	///
581	/// # PTX
582	///
583	/// ```ptx
584	/// fence.mbarrier_init.release.cluster;
585	/// ```
586	///
587	/// # Safety
588	///
589	/// Must be called from within a cluster launch context.
590	#[inline(never)]
591	pub unsafe fn fence_mbarrier_init_release_cluster() {
592	    unreachable!("fence_mbarrier_init_release_cluster called outside CUDA kernel context")
593	}

597	/// # PTX
598	///

602	///
603	/// # Safety
604	///
605	/// Must be called from within a cluster launch context.
606	#[inline(never)]
607	pub unsafe fn fence_proxy_async_generic_release_shared_cta_cluster() {

609	        "fence_proxy_async_generic_release_shared_cta_cluster called outside CUDA kernel context"
610	    )
611	}

623	/// Must be called from within a cluster launch context.

635	/// Suspend the calling thread for approximately `ns` nanoseconds.
636	///
637	/// Used inside spin-wait loops to reduce memory bus contention and allow
638	/// pending asynchronous operations (like cluster-scope barrier arrivals)
639	/// to complete. The PTX ISA strongly recommends using this in
640	/// `mbarrier_try_wait` polling loops.
641	///
642	/// # Parameters
643	///
644	/// - `ns`: Approximate suspension time in nanoseconds. Values of 0x20–0x100
645	///   are typical. The actual delay is hardware-dependent and approximate.
646	///
647	/// # PTX
648	///
649	/// ```ptx
650	/// nanosleep.u32 N;
651	/// ```
652	///
653	/// # Safety
654	///
655	/// Must be called from within a CUDA kernel context.
656	#[inline(never)]
657	pub unsafe fn nanosleep(ns: u32) {
658	    let _ = ns;
659	    unreachable!("nanosleep called outside CUDA kernel context")
660	}

666	/// Arrive and immediately wait (combined operation).
667	///
668	/// Convenience function for the common pattern of arriving and then
669	/// waiting on the same barrier. Equivalent to:
670	///
671	/// ```rust,ignore
672	/// let token = mbarrier_arrive(bar);
673	/// mbarrier_wait(bar, token);
674	/// ```
675	///
676	/// # Safety
677	///
678	/// - `bar` must be initialized
679	/// - All arriving threads must call this for the barrier to complete

687	// Typestate-Based Managed Barrier API
688	// =============================================================================
689	//
690	// This section provides a safer, typestate-based API for barrier management.
691	// It prevents common mistakes like using a barrier before initialization or
692	// double-invalidation through compile-time type checking.
693	
694	use core::marker::PhantomData;
695	
696	// =============================================================================
697	// State Markers
698	// =============================================================================
699	
700	/// State: Barrier has been claimed but not initialized.
701	pub struct Uninit;
702	
703	/// State: Barrier is initialized and ready for arrive/wait operations.
704	pub struct Ready;
705	
706	/// State: Barrier has been invalidated and cannot be used.
707	pub struct Invalidated;
708	
709	// =============================================================================
710	// Kind Markers (users can define their own)
711	// =============================================================================
712	
713	/// Marker type for TMA-related barriers.
714	pub struct TmaBarrier;
715	
716	/// Marker type for MMA/tcgen05 compute barriers.
717	pub struct MmaBarrier;
718	
719	/// Marker type for general-purpose barriers.
720	pub struct GeneralBarrier;

722	// =============================================================================
723	// Barrier Token (Newtype)
724	// =============================================================================
725	
726	/// Token returned from `arrive()`, must be passed to `wait()`.
727	///
728	/// This newtype prevents accidentally passing raw `u64` values
729	/// where a barrier token is expected.
730	///
731	/// # Example
732	///
733	/// ```rust,ignore
734	/// let token = barrier.arrive();
735	/// barrier.wait(token);  // Type-safe!
736	/// ```
737	#[repr(transparent)]
738	#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug)]
739	pub struct BarrierToken(u64);
740	
741	impl BarrierToken {
742	    /// Get the raw token value (escape hatch for advanced patterns).
743	    #[inline(always)]
744	    pub const fn raw(self) -> u64 {
745	        self.0
746	    }
747	
748	    /// Create from a raw value (for interop with low-level APIs).
749	    #[inline(always)]
750	    pub const fn from_raw(raw: u64) -> Self {
751	        Self(raw)
752	    }
753	}

755	// =============================================================================
756	// ManagedBarrier
757	// =============================================================================
758	
759	/// A barrier with typestate lifecycle management.
760	///
761	/// This type uses Rust's type system to enforce correct barrier usage:
762	/// - Cannot `arrive()` on an uninitialized barrier
763	/// - Cannot double-initialize
764	/// - Cannot use after invalidation
765	/// - `inval()` consumes the barrier, preventing reuse
766	///
767	/// # Type Parameters
768	///
769	/// - `State`: Current lifecycle state (`Uninit`, `Ready`, `Invalidated`)
770	/// - `Kind`: Marker type distinguishing different barriers (`TmaBarrier`, `MmaBarrier`, etc.)
771	/// - `ID`: Const generic index for multiple barriers of the same kind (default 0)
772	///
773	/// # Thread Requirements
774	///
775	/// | Operation       | Thread Requirement        |
776	/// |-----------------|---------------------------|
777	/// | `from_static()` | Single thread (thread 0)  |
778	/// | `init()`        | Single thread (thread 0)  |
779	/// | `arrive()`      | All participating threads |
780	/// | `wait()`        | All participating threads |
781	/// | `inval()`       | Single thread (thread 0)  |
782	///
783	/// # Example
784	///
785	/// ```rust,ignore
786	/// // Thread 0: Create and initialize
787	/// let bar = ManagedBarrier::<Uninit, TmaBarrier>::from_static(&raw mut BAR);
788	/// let bar = unsafe { bar.init(128) };  // Now Ready
789	/// fence_proxy_async_shared_cta();
790	/// sync_threads();
791	///
792	/// // All threads: arrive and wait
793	/// let token = bar.arrive();
794	/// bar.wait(token);
795	///
796	/// // Thread 0: Cleanup
797	/// sync_threads();
798	/// unsafe { bar.inval(); }
799	/// ```
800	pub struct ManagedBarrier<State, Kind, const ID: usize = 0> {
801	    ptr: *const Barrier,
802	    _state: PhantomData<State>,
803	    _kind: PhantomData<Kind>,
804	}
805	
806	// Safety: Barrier pointer is only accessed through synchronized operations
807	unsafe impl<S, K, const ID: usize> Send for ManagedBarrier<S, K, ID> {}
808	
809	// =============================================================================
810	// Uninit State Implementation
811	// =============================================================================
812	
813	impl<Kind, const ID: usize> ManagedBarrier<Uninit, Kind, ID> {
814	    /// Create an Uninit barrier from an explicit static declaration.
815	    ///
816	    /// Wrap a `static mut Barrier` in the typestate wrapper. Each barrier
817	    /// must be declared as a separate `static mut` variable, following the
818	    /// same pattern as `SharedArray`.
819	    ///
820	    /// # Thread Requirements
821	    ///
822	    /// **Single-thread**: Only call from the initialization thread (thread 0).
823	    ///
824	    /// # Example
825	    ///
826	    /// ```rust,ignore
827	    /// static mut BAR0: Barrier = Barrier::UNINIT;
828	    /// static mut BAR1: Barrier = Barrier::UNINIT;
829	    ///
830	    /// if tid == 0 {
831	    ///     let bar0 = ManagedBarrier::<Uninit, TmaBarrier>::from_static(&raw mut BAR0);
832	    ///     let bar1 = ManagedBarrier::<Uninit, GeneralBarrier>::from_static(&raw mut BAR1);
833	    ///
834	    ///     let bar0 = unsafe { bar0.init(32) };
835	    ///     let bar1 = unsafe { bar1.init(32) };
836	    /// }
837	    /// ```
838	    pub fn from_static(ptr: *mut Barrier) -> Self {
839	        ManagedBarrier {
840	            ptr,
841	            _state: PhantomData,
842	            _kind: PhantomData,
843	        }
844	    }
845	
846	    /// Initialize the barrier with an expected arrival count.
847	    ///
848	    /// **All threads in the block should call this.** Only thread 0 performs
849	    /// the actual initialization; all threads synchronize and receive a `Ready` handle.
850	    ///
851	    /// This is a convenience wrapper for `init_by(count, 0)`.
852	    ///
853	    /// # Block-Scoped Barriers
854	    ///
855	    /// mbarrier operates at **block scope** - each block has its own barrier in
856	    /// shared memory. Exactly ONE thread per block must call `mbarrier_init`.
857	    ///
858	    /// # Safety
859	    ///
860	    /// - Must be called before any arrive/wait operations
861	    /// - All participating threads in the block must call this together
862	    ///
863	    /// # Example
864	    ///
865	    /// ```rust,ignore
866	    /// static mut BAR: Barrier = Barrier::UNINIT;
867	    ///
868	    /// // ALL threads call init - only thread 0 actually initializes
869	    /// let bar = ManagedBarrier::<Uninit, TmaBarrier>::from_static(&raw mut BAR);
870	    /// let bar = unsafe { bar.init(128) };  // All threads get Ready handle
871	    /// ```
872	    #[inline(always)]
873	    pub unsafe fn init(self, count: u32) -> ManagedBarrier<Ready, Kind, ID> {
874	        unsafe { self.init_by(count, 0) }
875	    }
876	
877	    /// Initialize the barrier with a specific thread performing initialization.
878	    ///
879	    /// **All threads in the block should call this.** Only the thread with
880	    /// `threadIdx.x == init_thread` performs the actual initialization;
881	    /// all threads synchronize and receive a `Ready` handle.
882	    ///
883	    /// # Block-Scoped Barriers
884	    ///
885	    /// mbarrier operates at **block scope** - each block has its own barrier in
886	    /// shared memory. Exactly ONE thread per block must call `mbarrier_init`.
887	    /// Any thread can be the initializer, not just thread 0.
888	    ///
889	    /// # Parameters
890	    ///
891	    /// - `count`: Expected number of arrivals before barrier completes
892	    /// - `init_thread`: Thread ID (threadIdx.x) that performs initialization
893	    ///
894	    /// # Safety
895	    ///
896	    /// - Must be called before any arrive/wait operations
897	    /// - All participating threads in the block must call this together
898	    /// - `init_thread` must be a valid thread ID within the block
899	    ///
900	    /// # Example
901	    ///
902	    /// ```rust,ignore
903	    /// static mut BAR: Barrier = Barrier::UNINIT;
904	    ///
905	    /// // Use thread 31 (last thread in first warp) as initializer
906	    /// let bar = ManagedBarrier::<Uninit, TmaBarrier>::from_static(&raw mut BAR);
907	    /// let bar = unsafe { bar.init_by(128, 31) };
908	    /// ```
909	    #[inline(always)]
910	    pub unsafe fn init_by(self, count: u32, init_thread: u32) -> ManagedBarrier<Ready, Kind, ID> {
911	        if crate::thread::threadIdx_x() == init_thread {
912	            unsafe {
913	                mbarrier_init(self.ptr as *mut Barrier, count);
914	                fence_proxy_async_shared_cta();
915	            }
916	        }
917	        // All threads synchronize - ensures init is visible to all
918	        crate::thread::sync_threads();
919	
920	        ManagedBarrier {
921	            ptr: self.ptr,
922	            _state: PhantomData,
923	            _kind: PhantomData,
924	        }
925	    }
926	}

928	// =============================================================================
929	// Ready State Implementation
930	// =============================================================================
931	
932	impl<Kind, const ID: usize> ManagedBarrier<Ready, Kind, ID> {
933	    /// Get the raw pointer to the underlying barrier.
934	    ///
935	    /// Useful for interop with low-level APIs.
936	    #[inline(always)]
937	    pub fn as_ptr(&self) -> *const Barrier {
938	        self.ptr
939	    }
940	
941	    /// Arrive at the barrier.
942	    ///
943	    /// Returns a token that must be passed to `wait()` or `try_wait()`.
944	    ///
945	    /// # Thread Requirements
946	    ///
947	    /// All participating threads must call this.
948	    #[inline(always)]
949	    pub fn arrive(&self) -> BarrierToken {
950	        unsafe { BarrierToken(mbarrier_arrive(self.ptr)) }
951	    }
952	
953	    /// Arrive at the barrier expecting TMA transaction bytes.
954	    ///
955	    /// Use when this barrier tracks TMA copy completion. The barrier won't
956	    /// complete until both all arrivals occur AND all expected bytes transfer.
957	    ///
958	    /// # Thread Requirements
959	    ///
960	    /// **Single-thread**: The thread that issued the TMA copy.
961	    #[inline(always)]
962	    pub fn arrive_expect_tx(&self, bytes: u32) -> BarrierToken {
963	        unsafe { BarrierToken(mbarrier_arrive_expect_tx(self.ptr, 1, bytes)) }
964	    }
965	
966	    /// Wait for barrier completion (blocking).
967	    ///
968	    /// Blocks until all expected arrivals have occurred.
969	    ///
970	    /// # Thread Requirements
971	    ///
972	    /// All participating threads should wait.
973	    #[inline(always)]
974	    pub fn wait(&self, token: BarrierToken) {
975	        unsafe { mbarrier_wait(self.ptr, token.0) }
976	    }
977	
978	    /// Try to wait for barrier completion (non-blocking).
979	    ///
980	    /// Returns `true` if the barrier phase is complete, `false` otherwise.
981	    /// Preferred over busy-looping on `test_wait` due to better scheduling hints.
982	    #[inline(always)]
983	    pub fn try_wait(&self, token: BarrierToken) -> bool {
984	        unsafe { mbarrier_try_wait(self.ptr, token.0) }
985	    }
986	
987	    /// Test if barrier phase is complete (non-blocking).
988	    #[inline(always)]
989	    pub fn test_wait(&self, token: BarrierToken) -> bool {
990	        unsafe { mbarrier_test_wait(self.ptr, token.0) }
991	    }
992	
993	    /// Try wait using parity (for tcgen05.commit patterns).
994	    ///
995	    /// Use when the producer arrives via operations that don't return tokens
996	    /// (like `tcgen05_commit`).
997	    #[inline(always)]
998	    pub fn try_wait_parity(&self, parity: u32) -> bool {
999	        unsafe { mbarrier_try_wait_parity(self.ptr, parity) }
1000	    }
1001	
1002	    /// Invalidate the barrier.
1003	    ///
1004	    /// **All threads in the block should call this.** Only thread 0 performs
1005	    /// the actual invalidation; all threads synchronize before returning.
1006	    ///
1007	    /// This is a convenience wrapper for `inval_by(0)`.
1008	    ///
1009	    /// Consumes the `Ready` barrier and returns an `Invalidated` barrier.
1010	    /// The underlying memory can be reused after this.
1011	    ///
1012	    /// # Safety
1013	    ///
1014	    /// - All threads must have completed their wait operations before calling
1015	    /// - All participating threads in the block must call this together
1016	    ///
1017	    /// # Example
1018	    ///
1019	    /// ```rust,ignore
1020	    /// // ALL threads call inval - only thread 0 actually invalidates
1021	    /// let _dead = unsafe { bar.inval() };  // Consumes Ready, returns Invalidated
1022	    /// ```
1023	    #[inline(always)]
1024	    pub unsafe fn inval(self) -> ManagedBarrier<Invalidated, Kind, ID> {
1025	        unsafe { self.inval_by(0) }
1026	    }
1027	
1028	    /// Invalidate the barrier with a specific thread performing invalidation.
1029	    ///
1030	    /// **All threads in the block should call this.** Only the thread with
1031	    /// `threadIdx.x == inval_thread` performs the actual invalidation;
1032	    /// all threads synchronize before returning.
1033	    ///
1034	    /// Consumes the `Ready` barrier and returns an `Invalidated` barrier.
1035	    ///
1036	    /// # Parameters
1037	    ///
1038	    /// - `inval_thread`: Thread ID (threadIdx.x) that performs invalidation
1039	    ///
1040	    /// # Safety
1041	    ///
1042	    /// - All threads must have completed their wait operations before calling
1043	    /// - All participating threads in the block must call this together
1044	    /// - `inval_thread` must be a valid thread ID within the block
1045	    ///
1046	    /// # Example
1047	    ///
1048	    /// ```rust,ignore
1049	    /// // Use thread 31 as the invalidator
1050	    /// let _dead = unsafe { bar.inval_by(31) };
1051	    /// ```
1052	    #[inline(always)]
1053	    pub unsafe fn inval_by(self, inval_thread: u32) -> ManagedBarrier<Invalidated, Kind, ID> {
1054	        // Ensure all threads are done with the barrier before invalidating
1055	        crate::thread::sync_threads();
1056	
1057	        if crate::thread::threadIdx_x() == inval_thread {
1058	            unsafe { mbarrier_inval(self.ptr as *mut Barrier) };
1059	        }
1060	
1061	        // All threads synchronize - ensures inval is complete
1062	        crate::thread::sync_threads();
1063	
1064	        ManagedBarrier {
1065	            ptr: self.ptr,
1066	            _state: PhantomData,
1067	            _kind: PhantomData,
1068	        }
1069	    }
1070	}

6	#![allow(non_snake_case)]
7	//! Thread Block Cluster intrinsics for Hopper (sm_90+).
8	//!
9	//! Thread Block Clusters are a new hierarchy level introduced in NVIDIA Hopper
10	//! that groups multiple thread blocks together. Blocks within a cluster can:
11	//!
12	//! 1. **Directly access each other's shared memory** (Distributed Shared Memory)
13	//! 2. **Synchronize at cluster granularity** (faster than grid-wide sync)
14	//! 3. **Coordinate TMA operations** across blocks
15	//!
16	//! # Hierarchy
17	//!
18	//! ```text
19	//! Grid
20	//! └── Thread Block Cluster (NEW - sm_90+)
21	//!     └── Thread Block
22	//!         └── Warp
23	//!             └── Thread
24	//! ```
25	//!
26	//! # Hardware Requirements
27	//!
28	//! - Minimum: sm_90 (Hopper: H100, H200)
29	//! - Blackwell (sm_100/sm_120) extends cluster capabilities
30	//!
31	//! # Cluster Dimensions
32	//!
33	//! Clusters are defined as a 3D arrangement of thread blocks:
34	//! - Maximum total cluster size: 8 blocks
35	//! - `clusterDimX × clusterDimY × clusterDimZ ≤ 8`
36	//!
37	//! # Example
38	//!
39	//! ```rust,ignore
40	//! use cuda_device::{kernel, thread, cluster, SharedArray, DisjointSlice};
41	//!
42	//! #[kernel]
43	//! pub fn cluster_example(mut output: DisjointSlice<u32>) {
44	//!     static mut SHMEM: SharedArray<u32, 1> = SharedArray::UNINIT;
45	//!
46	//!     let tid = thread::threadIdx_x();
47	//!     let my_rank = cluster::block_rank();
48	//!     let cluster_size = cluster::cluster_size();
49	//!
50	//!     // Each block writes to shared memory
51	//!     if tid == 0 {
52	//!         unsafe { SHMEM.as_mut_ptr().write(my_rank * 100) };
53	//!     }
54	//!     thread::sync_threads();
55	//!
56	//!     // Sync entire cluster
57	//!     cluster::cluster_sync();
58	//!
59	//!     // Read neighbor's shared memory
60	//!     let neighbor = (my_rank + 1) % cluster_size;
61	//!     let neighbor_ptr = unsafe { cluster::map_shared_rank(SHMEM.as_ptr(), neighbor) };
62	//!     let value = unsafe { *neighbor_ptr };
63	//! }
64	//! ```
65

178	/// Get block's linear rank within cluster.
179	///
180	/// Computes: `cluster_ctaidX + cluster_ctaidY * cluster_nctaidX + cluster_ctaidZ * cluster_nctaidX * cluster_nctaidY`
181	///
182	/// This is useful for addressing blocks within a cluster, especially for
183	/// distributed shared memory operations.
184	///
185	/// # Returns
186	///
187	/// A value in range `[0, cluster_size)`.
188	#[inline(always)]
189	pub fn block_rank() -> u32 {
190	    let x = cluster_ctaidX();
191	    let y = cluster_ctaidY();
192	    let z = cluster_ctaidZ();
193	    let nx = cluster_nctaidX();
194	    let ny = cluster_nctaidY();
195	    x + y * nx + z * nx * ny
196	}
197	
198	/// Get total number of blocks in the cluster.
199	///
200	/// Computes: `cluster_nctaidX * cluster_nctaidY * cluster_nctaidZ`
201	#[inline(always)]
202	pub fn cluster_size() -> u32 {
203	    cluster_nctaidX() * cluster_nctaidY() * cluster_nctaidZ()
204	}

208	// =============================================================================

210	/// Synchronize all blocks in the cluster.
211	///
212	/// All threads in all blocks of the cluster must reach this barrier before
213	/// any thread can proceed. This is a cluster-wide barrier, similar to how
214	/// `sync_threads()` is a block-wide barrier.
215	///
216	/// # Usage
217	///
218	/// ```rust,ignore
219	/// // Each block writes to its shared memory
220	/// if thread::threadIdx_x() == 0 {
221	///     unsafe { SHMEM.as_mut_ptr().write(value) };
222	/// }
223	/// thread::sync_threads();  // Block-local sync first
224	///
225	/// // Cluster-wide sync - all blocks have written
226	/// cluster::cluster_sync();
227	///
228	/// // Now safe to read other blocks' shared memory via DSMEM
229	/// ```
230	///
231	/// # Safety
232	///
233	/// - All threads in all blocks of the cluster must reach the same barrier
234	/// - Placing `cluster_sync()` inside a conditional where not all threads enter
235	///   will cause deadlock
236	///
237	/// # PTX
238	///
239	/// Lowers to: `cluster.sync.aligned`
240	#[inline(never)]
241	pub fn cluster_sync() {
242	    unreachable!("cluster_sync called outside CUDA kernel context")
243	}

245	// =============================================================================
246	// Distributed Shared Memory
247	// =============================================================================
248	
249	/// Map local shared memory address to another block's address space.
250	///
251	/// This is the key primitive for distributed shared memory (DSMEM). Given a
252	/// pointer to this block's shared memory, it returns a pointer that can be
253	/// used to access the corresponding location in another block's shared memory.
254	///
255	/// # Parameters
256	///
257	/// - `local_ptr`: Pointer to shared memory in this block
258	/// - `target_rank`: Target block's rank within cluster (`0` to `cluster_size - 1`)
259	///
260	/// # Returns
261	///
262	/// A pointer that addresses the same offset in `target_rank`'s shared memory.
263	///
264	/// # Safety
265	///
266	/// - `local_ptr` must point to shared memory (not global, local, or constant)
267	/// - `target_rank` must be a valid block rank (`0` to `cluster_size - 1`)
268	/// - Must call `cluster_sync()` before accessing remote shared memory to ensure
269	///   the target block has written the data
270	///
271	/// # Example
272	///
273	/// ```rust,ignore
274	/// static mut SHMEM: SharedArray<u32, 256> = SharedArray::UNINIT;
275	///
276	/// // Write to our shared memory
277	/// unsafe { SHMEM.as_mut_ptr().add(tid).write(value) };
278	/// thread::sync_threads();
279	/// cluster::cluster_sync();
280	///
281	/// // Read from neighbor's shared memory
282	/// let neighbor = (block_rank() + 1) % cluster_size();
283	/// let neighbor_ptr = unsafe { cluster::map_shared_rank(SHMEM.as_ptr(), neighbor) };
284	/// let neighbor_value = unsafe { *neighbor_ptr.add(tid) };
285	/// ```
286	///
287	/// # PTX
288	///
289	/// Lowers to: `mapa.shared::cluster.u32 %rd_dst, %rd_src, %r_rank`
290	/// (or `.u64` for 64-bit pointers)
291	#[inline(never)]
292	pub unsafe fn map_shared_rank<T>(local_ptr: *const T, target_rank: u32) -> *const T {
293	    // Prevent unused variable warnings in the unreachable stub
294	    let _ = local_ptr;
295	    let _ = target_rank;
296	    unreachable!("map_shared_rank called outside CUDA kernel context")
297	}
298	
299	/// Map local shared memory address to another block's address space (mutable).
300	///
301	/// Same as [`map_shared_rank`] but returns a mutable pointer for write access.
302	///
303	/// # Safety
304	///
305	/// Same requirements as [`map_shared_rank`], plus:
306	/// - Must ensure no data races when writing to remote shared memory
307	/// - Typically only one block should write to any given location
308	///
309	/// # PTX
310	///
311	/// Lowers to: `mapa.shared::cluster.u32 %rd_dst, %rd_src, %r_rank`
312	#[inline(never)]
313	pub unsafe fn map_shared_rank_mut<T>(local_ptr: *mut T, target_rank: u32) -> *mut T {
314	    let _ = local_ptr;
315	    let _ = target_rank;
316	    unreachable!("map_shared_rank_mut called outside CUDA kernel context")
317	}

319	/// Read a u32 value from another block's shared memory within the cluster.
320	///
321	/// Combines `mapa.shared::cluster` (address mapping) and `ld.shared::cluster.u32` (load)
322	/// into a single atomic operation. This is the correct way to read DSMEM — using
323	/// `map_shared_rank` followed by a pointer dereference generates a generic load
324	/// (`ld.b32`) which cannot access remote shared memory.
325	///
326	/// # Arguments
327	///
328	/// - `local_ptr`: Pointer to shared memory in the current CTA
329	/// - `target_rank`: Rank of the target CTA within the cluster (0 to cluster_size - 1)
330	///
331	/// # Returns
332	///
333	/// The u32 value at the corresponding offset in the target CTA's shared memory.
334	///
335	/// # Safety
336	///
337	/// - Must be called within a cluster launch context
338	/// - `local_ptr` must point to valid shared memory
339	/// - `target_rank` must be a valid rank (0..cluster_size)
340	/// - Target CTA must have written to this address before cluster_sync
341	///
342	/// # Example
343	///
344	/// ```rust,ignore
345	/// static mut SHMEM: SharedArray<u32, 1> = SharedArray::UNINIT;
346	///
347	/// // Write to our shared memory
348	/// if tid == 0 { unsafe { SHMEM.as_mut_ptr().write(my_rank * 100) } }
349	/// thread::sync_threads();
350	/// cluster::cluster_sync();
351	///
352	/// // Read from neighbor's shared memory
353	/// let neighbor = (block_rank() + 1) % cluster_size();
354	/// let value = unsafe { cluster::dsmem_read_u32(addr_of!(SHMEM) as *const u32, neighbor) };
355	/// ```
356	///
357	/// # PTX
358	///
359	/// Lowers to:
360	/// ```ptx
361	/// mapa.shared::cluster.u64 %rd_mapped, %rd_local, %r_rank;
362	/// ld.shared::cluster.u32 %r_result, [%rd_mapped];
363	/// ```
364	#[inline(never)]
365	pub unsafe fn dsmem_read_u32(local_ptr: *const u32, target_rank: u32) -> u32 {
366	    let _ = local_ptr;
367	    let _ = target_rank;
368	    unreachable!("dsmem_read_u32 called outside CUDA kernel context")
369	}

374	
375	/// Marker function for compile-time cluster configuration.
376	///
377	/// This is a compile-time configuration marker that tells the compiler to emit
378	/// `.reqnctapercluster` PTX directive for this kernel. It does NOT generate any
379	/// runtime code - it only configures the kernel's cluster dimensions at compile time.
380	///
381	/// # Usage
382	///
383	/// This function should NOT be called directly. Use the `#[cluster_launch(x, y, z)]`
384	/// attribute macro instead, which injects this marker:
385	///
386	/// ```rust,ignore
387	/// #[kernel]
388	/// #[cluster_launch(4, 1, 1)]
389	/// pub fn my_cluster_kernel(output: DisjointSlice<u32>) {
390	///     // Cluster of 4 blocks in X dimension
391	/// }
392	/// ```
393	///
394	/// # How It Works
395	///
396	/// 1. The `#[cluster_launch]` macro injects `__cluster_config::<X, Y, Z>()` at kernel start
397	/// 2. MIR importer detects this call and extracts the const generic parameters
398	/// 3. The marker call is NOT compiled - it's removed during compilation
399	/// 4. LLVM export emits `!nvvm.annotations` with `cluster_dim_x/y/z` metadata
400	/// 5. LLVM NVPTX backend emits `.reqnctapercluster X, Y, Z` in PTX
401	///
402	/// # PTX Output
403	///
404	/// ```ptx
405	/// .entry my_cluster_kernel .reqnctapercluster 4, 1, 1 { ... }
406	/// ```
407	#[inline(never)]
408	pub fn __cluster_config<const X: u32, const Y: u32, const Z: u32>() {
409	    // This function is detected at compile time and removed.
410	    // The const generics X, Y, Z are extracted to set cluster dimensions.
411	    // No runtime code is generated.
412	}
